coredata.dc:title: Aminoclay decorated with lanthanide complexes and carbon dots: Tunable emission and information encryption
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2019 Chinese Society of Rare Earths. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Chinese Society of Rare Earths. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Tianren
Yang, Jing
Li, Huanrong
Wang, Yige
coredata.dc:description: Herein we present emission color-tunable and multi-functional lanthanide(III) luminescent hybrid materials (Ln(DPA)@AC-CDs) by mixing aminoclay (AC), 2,6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid (DPA), Ln3+ (Ln = Eu, Tb or Eu and Tb in different molar ratios), and carbon dots (N,S-CDs) in water, showing high quantum yields up to 58.8%. The emission colors can be finely tuned by altering the excitation wavelength and the amounts of the components, and white light emission (CIE-(0.27, 0.25)) can be achieved for sample Eu1Tb2(DPA)@AC-CDs under 325 nm light irradiation. In addition, under 365 nm UV light excitation, the Eu(DPA)@AC-CDs powder exhibits red luminescence due to the sensitization effect of N,S-CDs on Eu3+, which turns to bright blue when the powder is dispersed in water attributed to the high dispersion of the aggregated N,S-CDs particles. These luminescent properties afford Ln(DPA)@AC-CDs potential candidates for designing optoelectronic devices like WLEDs or in information encryption applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lanthanide
Carbon dots
Hybrid material
White light
Information encryption
Rare earths

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of luminescent hybrid material Ln(DPA)@AC-CDs

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2019 Chinese Society of Rare Earths. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FT-IR spectra of AC (1), DPA@AC (2), Eu(DPA)@AC (3) and Eu(DPA)@AC-CDs (4) (powder samples).
Excitation spectra of Eu(DPA)@AC (a), Tb(DPA)@AC (b), Eu(DPA)@AC-CDs (c) and Tb(DPA)@AC-CDs (d) (powder).

Emission spectra of Ln(DPA)@AC-CDs with λ ex of 282 nm (a) and 325 nm (b) (powder); (c) CIE 1931 chromaticity coordinates of Ln(DPA)@AC-CDs (powder) with λ ex of 282 and 325 nm; (d) Eu1Tb2(DPA)@AC-CDs hydrogel under 325 nm UV light excitation.
5D0 decay curve of Eu3+ in Eu(DPA)@AC-CDs (red, λ ex = 282 nm, λ em = 615 nm) and 5D4 decay curve of Tb3+ in Tb(DPA)@AC-CDs (green, λ ex = 282 nm, λ em = 543 nm), respectively.

Temperature-dependent emission spectra of Eu (DPA)@AC-CDs (excited at 282 nm) (a), Tb(DPA)@AC-CDs (excited at 282 nm) (b) and Eu1Tb2(DPA)@AC-CDs (excited at 282 nm) (c) and their pH-dependent emission spectra, respectively (d–f).
Emission spectra of Eu(DPA)@AC-CDs with different addition amounts of N,S-CDs in powder state (a) and dispersed in water (5 wt%) (b), λ ex = 365 nm.

(a) Digital photos of the letters “HEBUT” (the abbreviation for “Hebei University of Technology”) derived from the filter paper by using the Eu(DPA)@AC-CDs aqueous dispersion (5 wt%) as the luminescent inks; (b) Digital photos of the Morse-coded bi-color microarray for “HEBUT” on filter paper by using the Eu(DPA)@AC-CDs aqueous dispersion (5 wt%) and the N,S-CDs aqueous solution (0.1 wt%) as the luminescent inks (red spots for “dots” and blue spots for “dashes”, respectively).

(a) Eu(DPA)@AC-CDs hydrogel (daylight); Ln(DPA)@AC-CDs hydrogels (b) and powders (c) with different molar ratios of Eu:Tb (1:0, 2:1, 1:2 and 0:1) under UV lamp (254, 302 and 365 nm) illumination; (d) The predicted 3D structure of Ln(DPA)@AC-CDs as well as the sensitization effect of DPA ligand and N,S-CDs to Eu3+.

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: Foundation item: Project supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21771050), Hebei Natural Science Foundation (B2017202048), Tianjin Natural Science Foundation (18JCYBJC17200), and Educational Committee of Hebei Province (GCC2014035).

head.ce:title: Aminoclay decorated with lanthanide complexes and carbon dots: Tunable emission and information encryption

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hebei Provincial Key Lab of Green Chemical Technology and High Efficient Energy Saving, School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Hebei University of Technology, Tianjin 300130, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hebei Provincial Key Lab of Green Chemical Technology and High Efficient Energy Saving
School of Chemical Engineering and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein we present emission color-tunable and multi-functional lanthanide(III) luminescent hybrid materials (Ln(DPA)@AC-CDs) by mixing aminoclay (AC), 2,6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid (DPA), Ln3+ (Ln = Eu, Tb or Eu and Tb in different molar ratios), and carbon dots (N,S-CDs) in water, showing high quantum yields up to 58.8%. The emission colors can be finely tuned by altering the excitation wavelength and the amounts of the components, and white light emission (CIE-(0.27, 0.25)) can be achieved for sample Eu1Tb2(DPA)@AC-CDs under 325 nm light irradiation. In addition, under 365 nm UV light excitation, the Eu(DPA)@AC-CDs powder exhibits red luminescence due to the sensitization effect of N,S-CDs on Eu3+, which turns to bright blue when the powder is dispersed in water attributed to the high dispersion of the aggregated N,S-CDs particles. These luminescent properties afford Ln(DPA)@AC-CDs potential candidates for designing optoelectronic devices like WLEDs or in information encryption applications.

Luminescent hybrid materials of hosting lanthanide complexes and carbon dots in aminoclay were successfully prepared, showing great potential in fabricating optoelectronic devices like WLEDs and in information encryption.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Encapsulation of lanthanide(III) (Ln3+) complexes (LCs) within matrices to prepare lanthanide organic-inorganic hybrid materials has been fascinating researchers for decades, since they exhibit both luminescent properties of LCs (e.g., pure emission colors, low self-absorption ratio and long excited state lifetimes) and high photo-/thermo-stability coupled with good processability. 1–7 The elaborate design of the luminescent hybrid materials by using various matrices, organic ligands, Ln3+ ions and other kind of luminophors offer tunable emission colors or stimuli-responsive capability, which make them suitable in luminescent sensors or information encryption. 8–14 However, their preparation is frequently compromised by drawbacks like complicated procedures and utilization of poisonous organic solvents. Therefore, it is highly desirable to develop much more simple and environmentally friendly methods.

Recently, aminoclay (AC), a talc-based layered organosilicate (Fig. S1), 15,16 has been recognized as an ideal matrix for preparing luminescent hybrid materials through ionic co-assembly between organic ligand and AC followed by coordination with Ln3+ under aqueous conditions, and the as-obtained materials show ability of forming luminescent hydrogel and high luminescence stability in water for long time, 17–19 because the amino groups of AC protect the luminescence of Ln3+ complexes from quenching by high-frequency OH oscillators of aqua ligands. 20–22 The main advantages are their easy and multi-functional design for diverse applications, as well as simple and environmental benign preparation. Nevertheless, up to now, such materials still have several shortcomings, for instance, the relatively low luminescence quantum yield, and lack of high intensity blue-light-emitting component for bright white luminescence generation. Considering the above mentioned factors, the effective organic ligands for sensitizing Ln3+ and new appropriate luminescent components are in high desire.

Herein we report new AC-based luminescent materials (denoted as Ln(DPA)@AC-CDs, Scheme 1 ) that developed by simple co-assembly of AC, 2,6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid (DPA, an efficient tridentate N,O-chelating organic ligand that can sensitize both Eu3+ and Tb3+) 23 and Ln3+ (Ln = Eu or Tb) in aqueous media followed by mixing with blue-emissive N,S-co-doped carbon dots (N,S-CDs). Not only the DPA ligand is able to sensitize Ln3+, but the N,S-CDs can also sensitize the luminescence performance of Ln3+. The hybrid materials show tunable emission colors from red to blue and white light (Eu1Tb2(DPA)@AC-CDs, λ ex = 325 nm) through variation of excitation wavelength and the amounts of the components, as well as good luminescence stability under aqueous conditions ascribed to the protection of DPA ligand and AC substrate. Intriguingly, under 365 nm UV light excitation, the emission color of Eu(DPA)@AC-CDs powder (red) turns to bright blue when the powder is dispersed in water attributed to the much higher dispersion of the aggregated N,S-CDs particles, making the Eu(DPA)@AC-CD potentially available for preparing aqueous luminescent inks in information encryption applications.

3-Aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES, 99%), MgCl2·6H2O (98%), 2,6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid (DPA, 99%), citric acid (98%), L-cysteine (99%), EuCl3·6H2O (99.9%) and TbCl3·6H2O (99.9%) were used as purchased, without further purification. AC and N,S-CDs were prepared by using the literature method. 9,17

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of luminescent hybrid material Ln(DPA)@AC-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: 0.2312 g of AC (0.2 mmol) was dissolved in 1 g of double-distilled water to form transparent hydrosol, then DPA (0.0167 g, 0.1 mmol) was added to the AC hydrosol followed by sonication for 5 min, resulting in translucent suspension (DPA@AC). After addition of 0.3333 g of 0.1 mmol/g LnCl3·6H2O aqueous solution to the DPA@AC suspension (DPA:Ln3+ = 3:1 in molar ratio), translucent hydrogel Ln(DPA)@AC (Eu(DPA)@AC, Eu2Tb1(DPA)@AC, Eu1Tb2(DPA)@AC and Tb(DPA)@AC) was formed immediately, and the Ln(DPA)@AC-CDs hydrogel was obtained by adding 1 mg of N,S-CDs (0.1000 g the 1 wt% aqueous solution) to the Ln(DPA)@AC hydrogel followed by shaking for a while and standing for several minutes. The powders of Ln(DPA)@AC-CDs and Ln(DPA)@AC were obtained by heating the corresponding hydrogel at 80 °C in air for 24 h. Besides, the AC hybrid composites containing only N,S-CDs or N,S-CDs and Ln3+ were also prepared (named as AC-CDs or Ln@AC-CDs) by using the same method for comparison with Ln(DPA)@AC-CDs.

The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of the powder samples were obtained on a Bruker Vector 22 spectrometer using KBr pellets for the powder samples, about 2 mg of each compound was mixed with KBr, finely ground and pressed into pellets. The luminescence (excitation and emission) spectra and the lifetimes were measured on an Edinburgh Instrument FS920P spectrometer, with a 450 W xenon lamp as the steady-state excitation source, a double excitation monochromator (1800 lines/mm), an emission monochromator (600 lines/mm) and a semiconductor cooled Hamamatsu RMP928 photomultiplier tube. The absolute luminescence quantum yields were determined on the Edinburgh Instruments FLS920P using a BaSO4-coated integrating sphere (diameter: 150 mm).

The N,S-CDs were prepared by using citric acid and L-cysteine as raw materials through a simple hydrothermal method and purified via dialysis method described in Ref. 4, showing bright blue luminescence under UV light excitation (Fig.S2(a)). The morphology and structure of the as-prepared CDs were confirmed by the transmission electron microscopy (TEM). TEM image (Fig. S3) shows that the particle size ranges from 3.0 to 5.8 nm, with an average size of 4.5 nm. By the corresponding high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of a single CD, a lattice plane can be clearly identified with spacing of 0.21, which corresponds to the (100) facet of graphitic carbon. 24 The functional groups (OH and COO–) of N,S-CDs can be revealed by FT-IR spectrum shown in Fig.S2(b). The luminescence spectra of N,S-CDs were also measured, as shown in Fig.S2(c), the broad absorption band from 280 to 400 nm centered at 354 nm is mainly due to the trapping of excited state energy by the surface states, resulting in intense blue luminescence with the maximum emission wavelength of 423 nm. 9,25 Besides, the highest luminescence quantum yields of N,S-CDs aqueous solution was determined to be 80.6%. These data are in good agreement with the literature results, indicating the successful preparation of N,S-CDs. 9,25,26 After dehydration at 80 °C for 24 h, the black N,S-CDs solid can be obtained, showing no typical blue luminescence under UV lamp illumination (Fig.S2(a)) due to the severe aggregation of the CDs nanoparticles in dry state. 9

The ligand DPA can be easily dissolved in the AC aqueous solution, non-covalently interacted with the aminopropyl groups of AC units, resulting in DPA@AC suspension, 15 adding aqueous solution of Ln3+ to which led to a rapid formation of the translucent hydrogel, Ln(DPA)@AC, and the corresponding powder was obtained by dehydrating the hydrogel in air, showing multi-colored lanthanide luminescence under UV lamp illumination (Fig. S4). After addition of N,S-CDs aqueous solution, the Ln(DPA)@AC hydrogel becomes white viscous suspension (Ln(DPA)@AC-CDs) and then recovers the gel state after standing for several minutes (Scheme 1(a)), and the powder of Ln(DPA)@AC-CDs is also obtained through dehydration of the hydrogel in air, which can form hydrogel again with the maximum water content of approximately 90 wt% (room temperature). Both the hydrogels and powders of Ln(DPA)@AC-CDs are highly luminescent (see Scheme 1(b) and (c)). Eu(DPA)@AC-CDs are mainly discussed in the main text. It is worth noting that the luminescence of Eu(DPA)@AC-CDs hydrogel is totally different from that in dry state under 365 nm UV light excitation, which was not observed in the previously reported N,S-CDs/Ln(DPA)3 mixture. 26

The interactions among the components of the hybrid materials are revealed by the FT-IR spectra (Fig. 1 ). The band peaking at 1615 cm−1 shown in Fig. 1(1) can be ascribed to the absorption of the amino groups of AC. In Fig. 1(2), the sharp peak at 1574 and 1389 cm−1 (Fig. 1(2)) is assigned to the asymmetric (as-COO–) and symmetric (s-COO–) stretching vibrations of the carboxylate groups of DPA, respectively, 27,28 while the peak at 1468 cm−1 (CN) belongs to the pyridine ring of DPA. 29 Comparing Fig. 1(3) with (2), the peaks of carboxylate groups and pyridine ring of DPA can be hardly observed after introduction of Ln3+, implying that coordination bonds between Ln3+ and DPA were formed, and the peak at 1615 cm−1 has also shifted to 1631 cm−1, which suggests that the amino groups of AC might also participate in coordinating with Ln3+. Apparently, the Ln3+ ions in the hybrid material are sufficiently protected by deprotonated DPA ligand as well as AC from coordinating with water molecules, 19,30 As a consequence, the hybrid materials exhibit high luminescence in aqueous system, which will be further discussed in the following part. The characteristic absorption bands of N,S-CDs could not be observed in the FT-IR spectrum of Eu(DPA)@AC-CDs (Fig. 1(4)), possibly due to the low concentration of CDs in the hybrid material.

The excitation spectrum of Eu(DPA)@AC was measured and is exhibited in Fig. 2 (a). A broad band ranging from 250 to 300 nm with the maximum excitation wavelength of 282 nm can be attributed to the absorption of DPA ligand, 31 while a strong sharp peak at 395 nm as well as several weaker peaks ranging from 350 to 470 nm are assigned to the intra-configurational excitation of Eu3+ ions. 18,29,32 The excitation spectrum of Tb(DPA)@AC-CDs (Fig. 2(b)) exhibits a broader DPA absorption band from 250 to 360 nm peaking at 282 and 315 nm, respectively, and strong self-absorptions of Tb3+ are not observed, indicating that the energy transfer efficiency from DPA to Tb3+ is higher than that from DPA to Eu3+. After addition of N,S-CDs to Ln(DPA)@AC, no obvious variations of the absorption band of DPA ligand peaking at 282 nm were observed (Fig. 2(c) and (d)). The emission spectra of Ln(DPA)@AC-CDs upon excitation at 282 nm were obtained and are shown in Fig. 3 (a). The sharp emission bands at 595, 615, 650 and 693 nm are ascribed to the 5D0 → 7F J (J = 1, 2, 3 and 4) transitions of Eu3+, with the 5D0 → 7F2 transition as the prominent one for the bright red luminescence, while the peaks at 492, 543, 582 and 620 nm are assigned to the 5D4 → 7F J (J = 6, 5, 4 and 3) transitions of Tb3+, which is dominated by the strongest 5D4 → 7F5 transition, and this line is responsible for the green luminescence. Tunable emission colors of the hybrid material can also be obtained by changing the molar ratio between Eu3+ and Tb3+ (Scheme 1 and Fig. 3(a)), and the corresponding CIE 1931 coordinates are shown in Fig. 3(c). In addition, we also measured the decay curves of 5D0 excited state of Eu3+ in Eu(DPA)@AC-CDs and 5D4 excited state of Tb3+ in Tb(DPA)@AC-CDs (Fig. 4 ), which can be well fitted according to the single-exponential function (I = I 0 + A*exp(−(t − t 0)/τ), R 2 > 0.998), suggesting that the average coordination environment of Eu3+ and Tb3+ in the hybrid material is uniform, 33 and the corresponding lifetime value was determined to be 1.58 ms for Eu3+ (τ Eu) and 1.71 ms for Tb3+ (τ Tb), respectively. Moreover, the luminescence quantum yields of the hybrid materials (Eu:Tb = 1:0, 1:2, 2:1 and 0:1) were measured to be 58.8%, 53.6%, 51.1% and 50.5%, respectively, which are higher than those of the previously reported AC-based materials. 17–19,32

By switching λ ex to 325 nm, the emission colors of Ln(DPA)@AC-CDs changed, and white luminescence can be observed for Eu1Tb2(DPA)@AC-CDs (Scheme 1), which was not found in Ln(DPA)@AC (Fig. S5). We noticed that the blue emission band of N,S-CDs centered at 423 nm can be observed in the emission spectra of Ln(DPA)@AC-CDs and Ln@AC-CDs powders (Fig. 3(b) and Fig. S7), implying that the AC clay can prevent the aggregation of CDs in Eu1Tb2(DPA)@AC-CDs. 34 In order to confirm such effect, we measured the emission spectrum of AC-CDs powder, as shown in Fig. S6. Apparently, the AC-CDs exhibits much stronger blue emission than that of pure N,S-CDs in dry state. Similar results have also been reported in literature. 9,35,36 The absorption of the CDs at 282 nm is much weaker than that at 325 nm (Fig. S2(c)), thus the characteristic emission band of the CDs was not observed in the emission spectra of Ln(DPA)@AC-CDs excited at 280 nm (Fig. 3(a)). The CIE 1931 coordinates of the hybrid materials at λ ex of 325 nm are shown in Fig. 3(c), and the Eu1Tb2(DPA)@AC-CDs exhibit white coordinate located at (0.27, 0.25), as evidenced by the digital photo of the hydrogel taken under 325 nm UV light excitation (Fig. 3(d)). These features make the hybrid materials potentially available for fabricating optoelectronic devices like WLEDs. Besides, the luminescent behavior of the hybrid materials can also be tuned by temperature variation. The temperature dependent luminescence spectra of Eu(DPA)@AC-CDs, Tb(DPA)@AC-CDs and Eu1Tb2(DPA)@AC-CDs (77–297 K, ΔT = 20 K) are shown in Fig. 5 (a–c), respectively. We can see that the emission intensity decreases with the temperature increasing. In addition, the effect of pH on the luminescent behavior has also been studied. We can conclude that the luminescent behavior of the hybrid materials is sensitive to pH from Fig. 5(d–f).

An intriguing phenomenon is that the Eu(DPA)@AC-CDs powder exhibits red luminescence at λ ex of 365 nm, while the Eu(DPA)@AC powder does not (Scheme 1 and Fig. S4). We notice that a relatively weaker and broad absorption band ranging from 310 to 410 nm emerged in the excitation spectrum of Eu(DPA)@AC-CDs (Fig. 2(c)), which is similar to that of N,S-CDs shown in Fig. S2(c). Furthermore, we measured the excitation spectrum of Eu@AC-CDs by monitoring the Eu3+ 5D0 → 7F2 transition line at 615 nm, as shown in Fig. S6(a), in which the afore-mentioned broad absorption band can also be observed. The emission spectrum of Eu@AC-CDs (Fig. S6(b)) also exhibits the characteristic emission bands of Eu3+, as dominated by the 5D0 → 7F2 transition line of Eu3+ (615 nm), and the 5D0 lifetime of Eu3+ in Eu@AC-CDs was measured to be 0.32 ms (Fig. S6(c)). This reveals that the N,S-CDs is also capable of sensitizing Eu3+ ions for emitting their characteristic luminescence rather than be simply superposed with the Eu3+ complexes. To further investigate the sensitization effect of N,S-CDs on Eu3+ in the hybrid material, we measured the lifetimes of N,S-CDs in Eu@AC-CDs and AC-CDs, as shown in Fig. S7. Obviously, the lifetime of N,S-CDs decreases from 2.21 to 1.73 ns with the existence of Eu3+ in the hybrid material, suggesting the energy transfer process from N,S-CDs to Eu3+, and the energy transfer efficiency (η CDs-Eu) was calculated to be 21.7% according to the equation (η = 1–τ/τ 0). 37 Similar sensitization effect of N,S-CDs on Tb3+ in the hybrid material was also observed (Fig. 2(d) and Fig. S6(d–f)). However, the emission colors of Tb(DPA)@AC-CDs in wet and dry states are not remarkably different (Scheme 1), therefore its luminescent properties at λ ex of 365 nm were not further explored in this study.

Luminescent inks play a significant role in anti-counterfeiting and information encryption, 10,26,38 and the high emission color contrast of Eu(DPA)@AC-CDs in wet and dry states makes the hybrid material possibly available in these domains. Therefore, we prepared a series of Eu(DPA)@AC-CDs (powder) by varying the addition amount of N,S-CDs and studied their luminescence properties under 365 nm UV light excitation (Fig. 6 (a)). With the increasing addition amount of the CDs, the relative emission intensity of 5D0 → 7F2 transition of Eu3+ at 615 nm to the blue emission of N,S-CDs at 423 nm increased gradually, and similar variation tendency was also observed for the 5D0 lifetime of Eu3+ in Eu(DPA)@AC-CDs (λ ex = 365 nm, see Fig. S8). Interestingly, when the hybrid materials were dispersed in water, they exhibited much higher relative emission intensity of the CDs to Eu3+ than those of the powders (Fig. 6(b)). This should be attributed to the further high dispersion of the N,S-CDs particles after contact with water, which leads to much stronger blue emission. 9 Besides, the sample containing 3 mg N,S-CDs exhibits more pure blue luminescence than the initial one and was therefore selected for the next research.

Aqueous luminescent inks were prepared by simply dispersing the Eu(DPA)@AC-CDs in water (5 wt%), which was successfully loaded on the filter paper-based letters “HEBUT” (Fig. 7 (a)). Moreover, considering that the Morse code has long been used as the standard of information coding, we also transmitted the letters “HEBUT” into Morse-coded bi-color microarray on filter paper by using the above-mentioned luminescent inks as well as the N,S-CDs aqueous solution (0.1 wt%), as shown in Fig. 7(b), which only shows blue spots in wet state, and we can easily recover the valuable information by drying the spots. Besides, the information can be encrypted again by exposure to hot H2O vapor for several minutes. Such luminescent inks exhibit advantages like simple preparation and environmentally friendly operation, without using any toxic gases like HCl and Et3N. 10,39

In summary, hydrophilic, color-tunable and multi-functional lanthanide(III) luminescent hybrid materials (Ln(DPA)@AC-CDs) were successfully prepared by simply mixing AC, DPA, Ln3+ and N,S-CDs under aqueous condition. Except for the sensitization of DPA ligand to Ln3+, the N,S-CDs also act as sensitizer to Eu3+ and can influence the energy transfer efficiency from DPA to Tb3+. The hybrid materials exhibit bright and tunable luminescence from red to blue and white light (Eu:Tb = 1:2 in molar ratio, CIE-(0.27, 0.25)) by varying the excitation wavelength and addition amount of the components. In addition, the Eu(DPA)@AC-CDs powder exhibits red luminescence under 365 nm UV light excitation, which turns to bright blue when the powder is dispersed in water attributed to the absolute dispersion of the aggregated N,S-CDs particles. These properties afford Ln(DPA)@AC-CDs potential candidates for fabricating optoelectronic devices like WLEDs and for information encryption applications.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jre.2018.11.016.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multi-color luminescence in Eu3+/Tb3+ co-doped ZnAl amorphous materials and their annealed samples

Highly pure solid-state white-light emission from solution-processable soft-hybrids
Color-tunable luminescence of organoclay-based hybrid materials showing potential applications in white led and thermosensors
Multi-colored luminescent light-harvesting hybrids based on aminoclay and lanthanide complexes
A simple and green strategy for preparing luminescent Tb3+ complex-based nanocomposite with stable luminescence in water
Lanthanide ion luminescence probes of the structure of biological macromolecules

Carbon-based dots co-doped with nitrogen and sulfur for high quantum yield and excitation-independent emission
